entry.title: C0XMO Botnet Exploits CVE-2021-27137 as Silent Ransom Group Targets Law Firms
entry.content:
This coverage window highlights two distinct but operationally relevant threat streams: router-focused botnet expansion and human-driven data extortion against professional services. The reporting underscores continued attacker reliance on exposed edge devices, weak remote access credentials, social engineering, and legitimate administration tools to gain access and monetize intrusions. Fortinet researchers reported a new Gafgyt variant named C0XMO that targets DD-WRT router firmware through CVE-2021-27137, an unauthenticated buffer overflow vulnerability that can enable arbitrary code execution. The malware supports multiple CPU architectures, including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, SuperH, x86, and x8664, allowing it to operate across a broad range of embedded and router environments. C0XMO’s infection chain includes deployment of a Python scanner that searches for internet-facing systems, brute-forces weak Telnet and SSH credentials, identifies the target CPU architecture, and installs a compatible malware binary. Once deployed, the botnet copies itself into hidden locations, establishes cron-based persistence, and modifies shell startup files to support automatic execution after reboot or login. The malware also includes competitive botnet suppression behavior, terminating rival malware clients and tools that could interfere with its operation. After installation, C0XMO connects to a hardcoded command-and-control server and can receive commands for distributed denial-of-service activity, with 19 DDoS methods reportedly supported. Mandiant reported that Silent Ransom Group, also tracked as UNC3753, Luna Moth, and Chatty Spider, targeted dozens of legal, financial, and professional services organizations between January and May 2026. The campaign begins with invoice-themed phishing messages sent from consumer email accounts, followed by phone calls in which the attackers impersonate corporate IT personnel. The group’s intrusion workflow relies heavily on social engineering and legitimate remote access channels rather than traditional malware deployment. Victims are persuaded to join remote support sessions through tools such as Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Quick Assist, or Microsoft Terminal Services, after which attackers install remote monitoring and management software including AnyDesk, Zoho Assist, Bomgar, or SuperOps. Once inside victim environments, the attackers search for sensitive legal and financial documents and exfiltrate data using utilities such as WinSCP or Rclone. Mandiant reported that ransom demands often arrive within 30 minutes after the operators leave the environment, with victims typically given three days to respond.

Silent Ransom Group targets law firms with fake IT support calls — Should Read

Silent Ransom Group is targeting U.S. law firms with fake IT support calls that trick employees into granting remote access, enabling attackers to steal sensitive client and financial data within hours. The group now focuses on rapid data-theft extortion rather than ransomware encryption, using aggressive ransom demands and threats to contact clients directly.

  • Mandiant reported that the Silent Ransom Group, tracked as UNC3753, Luna Moth, and Chatty Spider, targeted dozens of legal, financial, and professional services organizations between January and May 2026.
  • The attacks begin with invoice-themed phishing emails from consumer email accounts, followed by phone calls in which attackers impersonate corporate IT staff.
  • Attackers convince employees to join remote support sessions through tools such as Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Quick Assist, or Microsoft Terminal Services and install remote monitoring and management tools such as AnyDesk, Zoho Assist, Bomgar, or SuperOps.
  • Once inside a network, the group searches for sensitive legal and financial documents and exfiltrates data using tools such as WinSCP or Rclone.
  • Mandiant said ransom demands often arrive within 30 minutes of the attackers leaving the victim environment and give organizations three days to respond.

C0XMO botnet spreads via DD-WRT router flaw, kills rival malware — Should Read

A new Gafgyt variant called C0XMO is exploiting a DD-WRT router flaw to infect devices across multiple CPU architectures, spread via scanning and weak SSH/Telnet credentials, and launch DDoS attacks. It also removes rival malware and persistence mechanisms, showing more sophistication than typical IoT botnets.

  • Fortinet researchers discovered a new Gafgyt variant called C0XMO that targets DD-WRT router firmware and supports multiple CPU architectures, including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, SuperH, x86, and x86_64.
  • C0XMO is delivered by exploiting CVE-2021-27137, an unauthenticated buffer overflow vulnerability that can lead to arbitrary code execution.
  • The botnet downloads a Python scanner that scans internet-facing systems, brute-forces weak Telnet and SSH credentials, detects CPU architecture, and deploys a compatible C0XMO binary.
  • After gaining access, C0XMO copies itself to hidden locations, creates cron jobs for persistence, and modifies shell startup files for automatic execution.
  • C0XMO can terminate competitor botnet clients and interfering tools, then connects to a hardcoded command-and-control server to receive commands including DDoS attacks using 19 supported methods.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two distinct but operationally relevant threat streams: router-focused botnet expansion and human-driven data extortion against professional services. The reporting underscores continued attacker reliance on exposed edge devices, weak remote access credentials,...

entry.title: NetSuite Invoice Phishing and Apache Solr CVE-2026-44825
entry.content: This coverage window highlights two distinct but related security themes: adversary abuse of trusted business infrastructure and exposure created by authentication weaknesses in enterprise platforms. The reported activity shows that technically valid authentication signals, whether email authentication or application login controls, can still coexist with high-impact risk when trust relationships or default credentials are misused. IRONSCALES reported an invoice phishing email delivered through legitimate Oracle NetSuite infrastructure, using the sender system@sent-via[.]netsuite[.]com on behalf of “Finance QIMA/WQS.” The message was relayed through Oracle Email Delivery from a Japan datacenter and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, demonstrating how phishing can evade controls that rely heavily on sender authentication and infrastructure reputation. The phishing lure used a “Click and Pay” call-to-action that directed recipients to a real QIMA payment portal containing a pre-filled payment token. This made the message appear aligned with normal invoice-payment workflows while shifting the risk to the embedded payment token and business-process context rather than an obviously malicious domain or attachment. Microsoft assigned the message SCL=9 with SFTY:9.25 despite successful authentication, indicating that downstream content and behavioral signals still flagged the email as highly suspicious. Themis classified the message as Invoice Phishing with 63% confidence based on sender relationship analysis and quarantined it across four mailboxes, underscoring the role of relationship and workflow context in detecting abuse of legitimate SaaS platforms. CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-44825, a hardcoded credentials vulnerability affecting Apache Solr’s Basic Authentication setup tool, bin/solr auth enable. The flaw has a CVSS v3.1 base score of 8.1 and affects Apache Solr versions 9.4.0 through 9.10.1 and version 10.0.0 in SolrCloud deployments where Basic Authentication was enabled using the affected tool. The issue arises when the setup process writes a security.json file containing template accounts named superadmin, admin, search, and index, each with a password matching the username. In affected deployments, authentication as superadmin can provide full administrative control over the SolrCloud cluster, including the ability to read, modify, or delete indexes and alter cluster configuration. Apache released Solr 9.11.0 and 10.1.0 to address CVE-2026-44825. The reporting also notes that organizations unable to immediately upgrade can identify exposure by examining security.json for the template accounts, making the vulnerability particularly relevant to internet-exposed or broadly accessible SolrCloud environments where administrative authentication is reachable.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two distinct but related security themes: adversary abuse of trusted business infrastructure and exposure created by authentication weaknesses in enterprise platforms. The reported activity shows that technically valid authentication signals, whether email...

entry.title: External Attack Surface Blind Spots and AI-Driven Remediation Pressure
entry.content: The coverage window is dominated by commentary on external attack surface exposure and the operational gap between known vulnerability management processes and attacker-visible assets. The central theme is that organizations increasingly face risk from internet-facing systems they do not know they own, while AI-enabled attacker workflows are compressing the time available to identify and remediate critical exposures. CyCognito CEO Rob Gurzeev highlighted that some of the greatest external attack surface risk comes from complete organizational blind spots, including applications, devices, cloud resources, and other internet-facing assets that are unknown to security teams. He distinguished traditional vulnerability management, which focuses on mapping potential vulnerabilities, from external attack surface management, which attempts to map, classify, and understand exposed assets from the perspective of an outside attacker. The article frames this issue as a scale problem for large enterprises. It states that large organizations may have between 100,000 applications, devices, and cloud assets exposed to the internet, while CyCognito’s largest customer reportedly has 100 million internet-connected assets. For threat hunters, SOC analysts, and exposure management teams, the reported scale underscores how asset inventory gaps can become a primary driver of unmanaged risk. Gurzeev also linked the growth of AI-enabled attacker capabilities to increasing pressure on remediation timelines. The article states that AI is accelerating the need for organizations to reduce mean time to remediation for critical issues from days or months to hours, with one- to two-hour remediation windows presented as the emerging benchmark for critical exposures. The reporting positions this shift as part of a broader change in cyber defense strategy: organizations are being pushed to evaluate whether existing security processes and technologies can keep pace with faster discovery, exploitation, and decision cycles. The piece does not cite a specific intrusion campaign or CVE, but it reflects a wider trend in security operations toward attacker-perspective asset discovery and faster handling of externally visible critical issues.
entry.summary: The coverage window is dominated by commentary on external attack surface exposure and the operational gap between known vulnerability management processes and attacker-visible assets. The central theme is that organizations increasingly face risk from internet-facing systems they do not know they...

entry.title: CVE-2026-3300 Exploited to Take Over Everest Forms Pro WordPress Sites
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical WordPress plugin vulnerability affecting Everest Forms Pro. The reporting highlights a familiar high-impact web compromise pattern: vulnerable plugin functionality enabling code execution, followed by attacker-created administrator accounts for persistent site control. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-3300, a critical vulnerability in Everest Forms Pro versions 1.9.12 and earlier, to take over WordPress websites. The flaw resides in the plugin’s Complex Calculation feature, where submitted form values are inserted into a PHP code string and executed through PHP’s eval() function, creating a path to arbitrary code execution in vulnerable installations. According to Wordfence telemetry cited in the report, exploitation has been used to create rogue WordPress administrator accounts. One observed malicious account username was “diksimarina,” indicating that attackers are leveraging the vulnerability not merely for proof-of-concept activity but for direct administrative persistence on compromised sites. The vulnerability was submitted to Wordfence by researcher h0xilo in February, and the Everest Forms developer released a patch on March 18. Wordfence reported that active exploitation began on April 13 and that its firewall has blocked more than 29,300 exploitation attempts, underscoring broad scanning and exploitation activity against exposed WordPress deployments running affected plugin versions.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical WordPress plugin vulnerability affecting Everest Forms Pro. The reporting highlights a familiar high-impact web compromise pattern: vulnerable plugin functionality enabling code execution, followed by attacker-created...

entry.title: Miasma Worm Hits Microsoft Repositories and DocuSign Homoglyph Phishing Bypasses CDR
entry.content: This coverage window highlights two trust-abuse incidents affecting software development and enterprise messaging workflows. Both reports center on adversaries exploiting implicitly trusted channels: a compromised GitHub contributor account used to target AI coding agents, and an email security allow-list that permitted delivery of a multi-domain DocuSign-themed phishing message despite failed authentication signals. StepSecurity reported that on June 5, 2026, a malicious commit was pushed to the Azure/durabletask repository using a previously compromised contributor account. The commit added configuration files for Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, and VS Code that either executed or instructed execution of a credential-harvesting payload when the repository was opened, positioning the attack specifically against AI-assisted coding workflows. The report links this repository injection to the May 19 durabletask PyPI compromise through the same compromised contributor account. GitHub’s automated enforcement disabled 73 repositories across the Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations within a 105-second window. One operational impact was the disabling of Azure/functions-action, which caused GitHub workflows referencing Azure/functions-action@v1 to stop resolving and disrupted CI/CD pipelines for developers. The incident illustrates how a single compromised identity in a large open-source and cloud development ecosystem can cascade across repositories, package infrastructure, and dependent automation. IRONSCALES separately detailed a phishing message impersonating DocuSign using the display name “Vantage via D0cuSign,” substituting a zero for the letter O. The message showed significant domain inconsistency, with yshfoodsupply.com in the From address, hansabusad.com in the Return-Path, vantage.bank in the Reply-To, and candybreak.net in the X-Relaying-Domain header. SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and CompAuth all returned none or fail, but the message was delivered with SCL=-1 after passing through an allow-listed Votiro CDR relay at 44.206.213.130. The phishing email included a Mailchimp redirect call-to-action labeled “Review Document,” an invite.ics calendar attachment, and a document.png attachment served as image/jpeg. Themis scored the message at 66% confidence, identified the recipient as a VIP, and one mailbox was quarantined. Together with the Microsoft repository incident, the report underscores a recurring pattern in which adversaries rely less on novel malware and more on abusing trusted intermediaries, workflow automation, and security exceptions to reach high-value users and systems.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two trust-abuse incidents affecting software development and enterprise messaging workflows. Both reports center on adversaries exploiting implicitly trusted channels: a compromised GitHub contributor account used to target AI coding agents, and an email security...

entry.title: Miasma Hits Microsoft GitHub as CVE-2026-28318 Enters CISA KEV
entry.content: This digest is led by software supply chain risk, actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, and emerging evidence of AI accelerating vulnerability discovery. The most operationally significant items are the Miasma worm’s impact across Microsoft GitHub repositories, CISA’s addition of SolarWinds Serv-U CVE-2026-28318 to the KEV catalog, and large vulnerability disclosures affecting FFmpeg and Chrome. The Miasma self-replicating supply chain campaign reportedly affected 73 Microsoft GitHub repositories across the Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs organizations. GitHub disabled access to affected repositories, including Azure/azure-functions-host, citing a violation of its terms of service. The campaign included a re-compromise of the durabletask PyPI package, which had previously been infected by TeamPCP to deliver an information stealer on Linux systems. Miasma is assessed to be a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm that TeamPCP publicly released in mid-May 2026, indicating reuse or adaptation of publicly available worming tradecraft. Reported behavior includes pushing malicious code directly to GitHub repositories and wiring execution through developer tooling such as Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, VS Code, and the npm test script. This positions the campaign at the intersection of repository compromise, package ecosystem abuse, and AI-assisted developer workflow exploitation. CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U vulnerability, to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after evidence of active exploitation. The flaw is an uncontrolled resource consumption issue with a CVSS score of 7.5 that can allow an unauthenticated attacker to crash Serv-U using specially crafted POST requests with Content-Encoding: deflate. SolarWinds fixed the issue in Serv-U version 15.5.4 HF1 and described mitigations including limiting access to known addresses and blocking requests containing "content-encoding." CISA directed Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address the vulnerability by June 19, 2026. Separate vulnerability research highlighted the growing role of autonomous systems in bug discovery. Security startup depthfirst reported that an autonomous AI agent found 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg during a run costing about $1,000, with some flaws dating back 15 to 20 years, including a stack overflow in service-description-table code from 2003. Google also released Chrome 149 with fixes for 429 security vulnerabilities, including more than 100 rated critical or high severity. The most severe issue, CVE-2026-10881, is an out-of-bounds read and write in the ANGLE graphics engine that can allow a crafted page to escape the browser sandbox and execute code on the host. Research by Include Security and independent researcher Buchodi found that Bright Data’s iOS SDK can turn consumer devices into exit nodes for web-scraping traffic routed through users’ home internet connections. The analysis found that the peer channel carrying scraping jobs had no real authentication and that, on iOS, SDK traffic could bypass a configured VPN. Bright Data says its residential proxy supply includes consent-sourced IPs from an SDK embedded in free apps behind an opt-in screen. The reporting raises questions about whether disclosed consent accurately reflects SDK capabilities, citing a Roku app called Petflix whose opt-in screen described only occasional device and connection use while SDK settings allowed up to 200 GB of traffic per month. The report identified several Bright Data SDK domains associated with the relay function, including proxyjs.brdtnet.com and clientsdk.bright-sdk.com. The findings broaden the residential proxy risk model beyond traditional malware-driven botnets to include app-mediated monetization channels on consumer smart devices.
entry.summary: This digest is led by software supply chain risk, actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, and emerging evidence of AI accelerating vulnerability discovery. The most operationally significant items are the Miasma worm’s impact across Microsoft GitHub repositories, CISA’s addition of...

entry.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager CVE-2026-20245 Actively Exploited
entry.content: During this coverage window, reporting centered on active exploitation of a high-severity Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability with no patch currently available. The activity is notable because exploitation can lead to root-level command execution on affected management systems and, in limited observed cases, configuration changes propagated to SD-WAN edge devices. Cisco disclosed that CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager is being actively exploited. The flaw allows an authenticated local attacker with netadmin privileges to upload a crafted file and execute arbitrary commands as root on an affected system. The exploitation path is constrained by credential and access requirements: Cisco stated that successful exploitation requires valid credentials or prior exploitation of related vulnerabilities, specifically CVE-2026-20182 or CVE-2026-20127. This places CVE-2026-20245 within a broader intrusion chain in which attackers may first obtain access through other flaws or compromised accounts before escalating control over the SD-WAN management plane. Cisco reported limited cases in which exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 resulted in a configuration change being pushed to edge devices. That detail is significant for defenders because SD-WAN Manager occupies a central orchestration role, and unauthorized changes at the management layer can have downstream operational impact across distributed network infrastructure. At the time of reporting, Cisco said there were no patches or mitigations available for CVE-2026-20245. The company linked exposure to the broader vulnerability set affecting SD-WAN software and referenced fixes for CVE-2026-20182 as part of the surrounding risk context. The reported exploitation remains described as limited, but the vulnerability’s location in a network management platform increases its strategic importance. For threat hunters, detection engineers, SOC analysts, and incident responders, the key intelligence value is the combination of active exploitation, root-level execution potential, authenticated access requirements, and observed propagation of configuration changes to managed edge infrastructure.
entry.summary: During this coverage window, reporting centered on active exploitation of a high-severity Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability with no patch currently available. The activity is notable because exploitation can lead to root-level command execution on affected management systems and, in...
